era_context: The year 1472 falls within the late 15th-century transition in Europe, the Ottoman consolidation in the eastern Mediterranean, the Ming Dynasty's cultural flourishing in China, and the Joseon Dynasty's administrative development in Korea. In the Americas, the Aztec and Inca empires were at their territorial peaks. In Africa, Portuguese exploration was advancing along the coast; interior African states were developing independently of European contact.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Americas interior (no written records; archaeological evidence only)

events.title: Pazzi Conspiracy in Florence (preparation and context)
events.description: In 1472, tensions were building in Florence between the Medici family (dominant since Cosimo de' Medici) and rival families, particularly the Pazzi. Though the famous Pazzi Conspiracy would not occur until 1478, the political and economic rivalries that would fuel it were evident in 1472. The Medici's control of Florence's banking and political systems was generating resentment among competing merchant families. The year 1472 represents a period of underlying tension that would eventually erupt into violence.
key_figures: Lorenzo de' Medici (de facto ruler of Florence)
Jacopo de' Pazzi (patriarch of the Pazzi family)
Pope Sixtus IV (patron of the Pazzi)
events.certainty_note: The political tensions of 1472 are documented in Florentine records, though specific events are less precisely dated than the 1478 conspiracy itself.

events.title: Portuguese exploration reaches Cape Verde
events.description: Portuguese explorers, continuing the systematic exploration sponsored by the Portuguese crown, reached the Cape Verde islands in 1472. The Cape Verde archipelago, located off the coast of West Africa, became an important Portuguese trading post and later a center for the slave trade. The Portuguese established settlements and used the islands as a base for further exploration of the African coast. This expansion represented the consolidation of Portuguese maritime dominance in the Atlantic.
key_figures: Portuguese explorers and merchants (names often unrecorded)

sources.name: Portuguese royal charters and trading records
Gomes, Diogo. De Prima Inventione Guinee

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of Cape Verde is documented in royal records and later accounts, though the precise date of 1472 is approximate (±1–2 years).
cross_references: 1488-Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope

events.title: Chenghua Emperor's reign in Ming China continues
events.description: In 1472, the Chenghua Emperor (r. 1464–1487) continued his reign over the Ming Dynasty. The period was marked by cultural achievement, including the production of fine porcelain, landscape painting, and literary works. However, the Chenghua Emperor's reign was also characterized by the growing influence of eunuchs in court politics, which would have long-term consequences for Ming governance. The year 1472 represents a period of cultural flourishing but also the beginning of institutional problems that would weaken the dynasty.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Ming court records and archaeological evidence

events.certainty_note: Ming court records are extensive and contemporary. The Chenghua Emperor's reign is well-documented.

events.title: Joseon Dynasty under King Seongjong continues cultural development
events.description: In 1472, King Seongjong of Joseon (r. 1469–1494) continued his reign, overseeing the development of Confucian scholarship and the standardization of Hangul. The Joseon court was consolidating administrative structures and promoting literacy through the use of the Korean alphabet. The year 1472 represents a period of cultural and administrative maturation that would define Joseon's later achievements.

sources.name: Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Annals of the Joseon Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Joseon court records are detailed and contemporary.

events.title: Venetian-Ottoman War continues; Ottoman expansion in Balkans
events.description: In 1472, the First Venetian-Ottoman War continued with Ottoman forces consolidating control over the Balkans and Aegean. Venice was losing its traditional naval dominance and territorial holdings. The war exemplified the broader shift in Mediterranean power from Italian maritime republics to the Ottoman Empire. By 1472, it was clear that Venice's golden age of maritime dominance was ending.

sources.name: Venetian state records and Ottoman chronicles
Setton, Kenneth M. The Papacy and the Levant

events.certainty_note: The war's progression is well-documented in both Venetian and Ottoman sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of Portuguese exploration as a singular, triumphant European enterprise obscures the complexity of early contact. Portuguese traders were operating within existing African trade networks and were dependent on African cooperation and knowledge. The 'Age of Exploration' framing privileges European agency; recent scholarship emphasizes African, Asian, and indigenous American agency and the mutual nature of early contact. Additionally, the Medici family's dominance in Florence is sometimes presented as inevitable; modern scholarship emphasizes the contingency of their power and the genuine alternatives represented by rival families.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1472 is rich for Europe (especially Italy and the Mediterranean) due to extensive chronicle traditions and state archives. East Asian documentation is also rich. However, African and American histories are underrepresented in surviving written sources. The 'Age of Exploration' narrative privileges European perspectives; African agency and existing trade networks are less visible in European sources. Mesoamerican and South American histories are reconstructed from post-conquest Spanish chronicles and archaeological evidence, making precise dating of specific events difficult.

graph_edges.note: The political tensions of 1472 culminated in the Pazzi Conspiracy of 1478.
Portuguese exploration in 1472 was part of the systematic effort that culminated in rounding the Cape.
Ming and Joseon dynasties were contemporaneous regional powers with distinct but parallel cultural developments.
The 1472 Ottoman advances were part of the broader war that ended in 1479.
